codeReview.summary: The feature adds the requested rollback-flow assertions and OTEL coverage, but it does so by mutating the shared `~/repos/shuvbot-skills` checkout and then cleaning it with destructive commands. Because the test creates commits/tags, hard-resets the branch, and prunes untracked files in that shared repo, the implementation is not hermetic and can break or erase unrelated state on the test host.

issues.description: The test performs write-path rollback assertions directly against the shared `~/repos/shuvbot-skills` checkout instead of an isolated sandbox. It creates commits/tags and hard-resets that shared repo (`git commit`, `git reset --hard`, `git checkout`), which violates the mission/testing guidance to keep remote mutations inside isolated temp directories and makes the feature unsafe/non-hermetic.

Cleanup runs `git clean -fd` in the shared repo, which deletes all untracked files/directories rather than only the test-owned temp artifact. That can erase unrelated untracked state from other tests or users, and it directly contradicts the test's own comment that pre-existing untracked files may exist. The same destructive cleanup pattern is repeated in the OTEL path at line 478.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Shared guidance says remote write-path validation must use isolated test directories/sandboxes and must not mutate `~/repos/shuvbot-skills`, but the shipped E2E tests (including this feature) operate on that shared checkout and now require serialized execution to avoid interference.

The assigned `backend-worker` skill is package/service oriented and prescribes `packages/<name>/test/` TDD flows, but this feature was an E2E test under `tests/e2e` against live SSH hosts. The worker's own handoff notes that the TDD/red-phase guidance does not fit this kind of E2E work, so the skill appears mismatched for E2E features.

summary: Fail: the rollback test covers the intended flow, but it mutates the shared `~/repos/shuvbot-skills` checkout and uses destructive cleanup (`git clean -fd`) there, so the feature is unsafe and non-hermetic despite passing its own test run.
